body_txt: This Description of Absolute Comparative is three times more informative than the leading brand. Rather than tell us exactly how good the product is, advertisers often use slogans like  These sound positive but mean nothing. Smarter than what? How white does it wash? How are they measuring softness? Why do I want an "extreme" bathing sponge? By omitting the standard they're comparing their product against, they've avoided making specific claims about their product and made comparisons with their competitors much harder than otherwise. One of the more common forms of Weasel Words in advertising. Often, this happens because the country (e.g., the UK) prohibits it. Trademark laws can make it difficult to include competitor's names and especially logos or images of any of their products. Finally, it's been shown many times that mentioning other products in your adverts often has the rather undesirable effect of making the audience remember that in addition to, or even instead of, your own product. Compare Parity Product Paradox , where legally indistinguishable competitors are free to tout their claims to be "the best". See also Asbestos-Free Cereal , where the feature is simply a normal trait common to all similar products; 20% More Awesome , where the feature is a quality that cannot be objectively measured. In video games, they might pop up as a Cross-Referenced Description (one object's descriptive text doesn't make sense without another object). Vague Stat Values is for when gaming stats are unclearly compared. Tends to overlap with Lies, Damned Lies, and Statistics (using credible-sounding statistics for deception), as something can be the fastest-growing, but specifically compared to when or what? It's also seen in conjunction with Now How Much Would You Pay , because those never tell you which similar yet subpar stores and products would cost a fortune; and with Brand X . Sub-Trope of Meaningless Meaningful Words (sure, it sounds meaningful, but does it actually mean anything?). Contrast Strawman Product , in which the "better than" comparison is much more direct but equally exaggerated. See also Shaped Like Itself (tautologies that describe themselves as tautologies) and Our Better Is Different (subverting the idea of "higher is better").
